coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from prawn shells for highly selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
coredata.dc:creator: Gedda, Gangaraju
Lee, Chun-Yi
Lin, Yu-Chih
Wu, Hui-fen
coredata.dc:description: A facile, economical and effective green method was developed for synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) from prawn shells. The results showed that these C-dots (with an average diameter of 4nm) possess many excellent features such as to eliminate blue fluorescence under UV light (λ =365nm), with high monodispersity, good stability, excellent water solubility and high quantum yield (9%). We further explored these C-dots as effective sensing probes for Cu2+ detection and found that they exhibit excellent selectivity and sensitivity toward Cu2+ with a low detection limit of 5nM. We further demonstrated this novel sensing platform on Cu2+ ions analysis from seawater samples. This method is extremely rapid, low cost, ecofriendly, highly selective and sensitive for Cu2+ ions sensing from various sources of environment such as drinking water, river water and sea water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots
Copper sensing
Prawn shell
Inner filter effect
Green synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and mechanism of formation of C-dots from prawn shells
pH effect on fluorescence sensing system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV absorption spectra of the 20μg/mL C-dots solution (inset: photograph of the samples excited by normal light and a λ 365 UV lamp) and (b) emission spectra at various excitation wavelengths of the 20μg/mL C-dots.
FTIR spectra of the (a) chitosan and (b) C-dots.
(A) TEM micrograph, (B) particle size distribution histogram, (C) XRD pattern spectrum, (D) EDXS pattern of the C-dots.
The factors influence on C-dots fluorescence intensity. (a) The solution pH in the presence and absence of Cu2+. (b) Time effect C-dot–Cu2+ complexation.

Fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots after adding various concentrations of Cu2+ (from top to bottom: 0, 0.05, 0.075, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 2,3, 4 and 5). Inset: plots of intensity ratio (F 0/F) vs. the Cu2+ concentrations.

The difference in PL intensity of C-dots dispersion under various metal ions including Zn2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Ag2+, Mn2+ and Cd2+, [Mn+]=5μM. (The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements).

PL spectra of C-dots dispersion in the presence of different Cu2+ concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6 and 0.8μM) in seawater. Inset: plots of the values of (F 0/F)−1 vs. the concentrations of Cu2+.
Comparison of different nanomaterials/dye assisted analytical methods for Cu2+ sensing.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from prawn shells for highly selective and sensitive detection of copper ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Kaohsiung Medical University, Kaohsiung 807, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Kaohsiung Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Medical Science and Technology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Medical Science and Technology, National Sun Yat-Sen University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Doctoral Degree Program in Marine Biotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University and Academia Sinica, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Doctoral Degree Program in Marine Biotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University and Academia Sinica

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile, economical and effective green method was developed for synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) from prawn shells. The results showed that these C-dots (with an average diameter of 4nm) possess many excellent features such as to eliminate blue fluorescence under UV light (λ =365nm), with high monodispersity, good stability, excellent water solubility and high quantum yield (9%). We further explored these C-dots as effective sensing probes for Cu2+ detection and found that they exhibit excellent selectivity and sensitivity toward Cu2+ with a low detection limit of 5nM. We further demonstrated this novel sensing platform on Cu2+ ions analysis from seawater samples. This method is extremely rapid, low cost, ecofriendly, highly selective and sensitive for Cu2+ ions sensing from various sources of environment such as drinking water, river water and sea water.

ce:section.ce:para: Marine crustacean wastes such as squilla, krill, prawn, crab and lobster shells are industrial by-products. The worldwide seafood industries are estimated to generate approximately 60,000–80,000 tons of crustacean waste in one year [1]. The discarding of such large quantity of crustacean waste is a serious environmental problem especially because of they are resist to biodegradation, insoluble in water and to process the waste is a high cost [2,3]. The crustacean waste contains a large quantity of chitin (about 20–30% of body weight depending on the organisms) and small quantity of proteins, minerals (mainly calcium), lipids and pigments [4]. The chitin and its deacetylated derived components (including chitosan) have been widely used for various environmental purposes such as water treatment, paper production, anti oxidant, antibacterial and antifungal reagents. Moreover, chitosan has been widely used for detecting many heavy metals such as Cu2+, As2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, etc. due to their excellent chelating ability with heavy metals [5,6]. Therefore, the proper usage of the crustacean waste is not only to solve the disposal problems but also to serve as a new platform for many fields including industry, biomedicine, food processing, cosmetics and agriculture [7]. For sensible utilization of the marine crustacean waste, we developed carbon dots based sensors for sensing the copper(II) ions.

In recent decades, considerable efforts have been devoted to developing various analytical methods for sensing chemically and biologically important drugs, biomolecules, metal ions especially focused on the detection of toxic metal ions and organic pollutants [8–30]. Among these applications, the monitoring on copper ions has received increasing attention in toxicological point of view. It is often present in natural and environmental water such as seawater, river/lake water, drinking water and tap water [31,32]. Copper is an essential transition metal for human beings and also plays a crucial role in biological systems. However, it is severely toxic at higher concentrations with long-term of exposure with the human body. It can cause many neurodegenerative diseases such as Wilson's, Prion and Alzheimer diseases [33,34]. According to the U.S Environmental Protection agency, the upper limit of copper ions in drinking water is 20μM [35]. Therefore, it has extremely highly demand to monitor copper concentrations with high sensitivity and selectivity from the environment. Although, currently various analytical techniques are available for detecting copper including potentiometric sensors, electrochemical sensors, atomic fluorescence spectrometry, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [15,36–41]. They may detect copper with high sensitivity, but their applications are limited due to high cost, time-consuming, complicated sample preparation and inappropriate for real-time monitoring. As to the fluorescence assay methods have been used for sensing many metals with many advantages like high sensitivity, selectivity, simple sample preparation, rapidity and less interferences [42,43]. In recent years, many reports reveal that the fluorescent materials including semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes have been used for sensing metals [43–46]. However, these methods are limited with the following features. (1) Multiple synthesis and purification steps are required. (2) The nanomaterials are prepared from highly toxic and expensive chemicals such as precursors and surface modifiers. Thus, it is great demand to develop a new, a facile, rapid, inexpensive and green method for the production of biocompatible fluorescent nanomaterials.

Xu et al. [47] accidently found carbon dots (C-dots) during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes by electrophoresis in 2004. C-dots gained considerable attention due to their unique physical, chemical, optical and photoluminescence properties [48]. Compared to the conventional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, the fluorescent C-dots are superior in many aspects especially in simple and rapid synthesis process, excellent water solubility, high biocompatibility, less toxicity and high photostability. Because of these outstanding properties, they can be used to replace the toxic quantum dots in many clinical and environmental applications [49,50]. So far, a variety of synthesis methods toward C-dots have been developed such as laser ablation, arc discharge, electrochemical oxidation, microwave and hydrothermal [51–54]. However, they are not ideal choices, because most of these approaches suffer from some disadvantages like the use of a high quantity of strong acids, low quantum yield and involvement of complex process. Moreover, additional expensive surface passivating agents were used to enhance their fluorescence property and selective sensing of metals [55]. From the environment, material synthesis and sensing point of views, the study of new carbon source for facile, economical, simple and green synthesis of such C-dots from waste materials without additional surface passive agent is highly desired.

Here, we demonstrate the use of cost-free seafood waste as the new carbon source for the green synthesis of water-soluble fluorescents C-dots without using any surface passive agent. This method can recycle the environmental wastes for the preparation of C-dots. We further demonstrated that seafood waste derived C-dots can serve as label-free fluorescent sensors for selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+. To demonstrate the practical application of this fluorescence sensing system, it is further applied to monitor Cu2+ ions from seawater.

TEM images of C-dots were recorded by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM-3010, JEOL, Japan). The UV–visible spectrum of C-dots was recorded by using double beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-3501, Japan). The fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots were recorded by using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-2700, and Japan). The elemental composition of C-dot was observed by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDXS, Oxford Instruments, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by XRD spectroscopy (Bruker D8 Advance, Germany). C-dots functional groups investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker FT-IR IFS-48, Germany).

Prawn shells were bought from Kaohsiung local market, Taiwan. The prawn shell was washed with tap water and followed by deionized water. The cleaned samples were dried overnight at 80°C. The dried shells were ground by mortar and pestle to make the fine powder. Seawater was collected from Shiziwan Bay, Kaohsiung, Taiwan. The seawater samples were filtered through the 0.22μm membranes (Millipore, USA) prior to the detection.

2.0g of dried prawn shell powder was treated with 25mL of 3% NaOH solution at room temperature for 5h. The powder washed with deionized water until a neutral pH was obtained. The sample was treated with 1% hydrochloric acid solution for 6h at room temperature. After acid treatment, the sample was washed with deionized water by several times to obtain a neutral pH. The prawn shells were transferred to a 50% sodium hydroxide solution and refluxed at 80°C for 2h. The refluxed sample was cooled down to the room temperature and filtered through with (Whatman) filter paper. The obtained product was dispersed in 25mL of 2% acetic acid solution. Then the sample was transferred to 45mL capacity stainless steel autoclave and kept at 200°C for 8h. The C-dots were collected by removing the large particles through centrifugation at 14,000rpm for 20min and finally dried under vacuum for overnight. The C-dots were dispersed in deionized water at a concentration of 1mgmL−1 for further characterization and use.

The quantum yield ( Φ ) of C-dots was estimated by comparing the integrated PL intensities and the absorbency values against the reference quinine sulfate. Quinine sulfate (from literature Φ
=0.54) was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (refractive index (η): 1.33),and the C-dots were dissolved in deionized water (η: 1.33).
Φ =
Φ r
I
I r
A r
A
η 2
η r 2

Here Ф is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index, and A is the optical density. The subscript r represents the reference fluorophore of known quantum yield.

The sensing of Cu2+ was performed at room temperature in PBS (0.2M, pH 4.0) solution. In a typical experiment, the desired amount of Cu2+ (final concentration 0–5μM) ions was added to 1mL of PBS solution congaing C-dot (final concentration 20μg/mL). The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 15min at room temperature. The reaction time, pH effect, sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted in triplicate measurements for all experiments.

The seawater sample was filtered through 0.22μm membrane after that centrifuged at 15,000rpm for 18min (at room temperature) prior to the detection. Aliquots (500μL) of this seawater samples were spiked with standard Cu2+ solutions (10μL, final concentration 0.2–0.8μM). The spiked samples were diluted to 1000μL with PBS (20mM, pH 4.0) containing C-dots (final concentration 20μg/mL) dispersion and then analyzed by the developed sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and mechanism of formation of C-dots from prawn shells
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the natural source of prawn shells biomass was used to synthesize the C-dots and further employed for sensing Cu2+. Naturally prawn shells are rich in various polar functional groups such as the amino group, secondary and primary hydroxyl group, rich polysaccharide, i.e., (poly-β(1→4)-N-acetyl-d-glucosamine) [56,57]. The procedures for the formation of C-dots from prawn shells are shown in Fig. S1. In the first step, pure chitin was obtained from prawn shells by the deproteinization with NaOH and followed by the demineralization process with HCl at room temperature. Here, proteins, other organic moieties and minerals were removed in deproteinization and demineralization steps. The resulting chitin was converted to chitosan via deacetylation by the chemical treatment with NaOH. Finally, the obtained chitosan was converted to C-dots by the hydrothermal carbonizations process.

In this work, the natural source of prawn shells biomass was used to synthesize the C-dots and further employed for sensing Cu2+. Naturally prawn shells are rich in various polar functional groups such as the amino group, secondary and primary hydroxyl group, rich polysaccharide, i.e., (poly-β(1→4)-N-acetyl-d-glucosamine) [56,57]. The procedures for the formation of C-dots from prawn shells are shown in Fig. S1. In the first step, pure chitin was obtained from prawn shells by the deproteinization with NaOH and followed by the demineralization process with HCl at room temperature. Here, proteins, other organic moieties and minerals were removed in deproteinization and demineralization steps. The resulting chitin was converted to chitosan via deacetylation by the chemical treatment with NaOH. Finally, the obtained chitosan was converted to C-dots by the hydrothermal carbonizations process.

The prepared C-dots solution was yellowish in color under normal light and exhibited intense blue luminescence under UV excitation (λ 365) (inset Fig. 1(a)). The optical and photoluminescence (PL) properties of these blue fluorescent C-dots were inspected using UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 1. From the UV–vis spectra, it can be seen that there are two absorption peaks, one at 280nm (sharpen absorption peak) and another around 330nm (flat peak) (Fig. 1(a)). These peaks are obtained due to π→π* and n→π electronic transition in C-dots surface [58]. We explored the PL properties of C-dots at different excitation wavelengths (270–390nm with an increase of 30nm), and the results are shown in Fig. 1(b). These findings indicate that the C-dots emission peaks are dependent on the excitation wavelength. Interestingly, the prawn shell derived C-dots showed up conversion from 390 to 405nm (at 270–330nm excitation) and down conversion from 405 to 440nm (at 330–390nm excitation). The maximum emission (405nm) was obtained with the excitation wavelength of 330nm. The maximum PL intensity of C-dots was observed at an excitation wavelength of 330nm, it may be due to the nature of C-dots surface and the high number of particles being excited at 330nm. The quantum yield of the C-dots was found to be 9% by using quinine sulfate as a reference quantum yield of C-dots in aq.solution and calculated at an excitation wavelength of 330nm.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed to investigate the C-dot surface, functional groups. Fig. 2 a shows the FT-IR measurements of chitosan powder and C-dots derived from prawn shells. The prawn shell derived chitosan exhibited a characteristic peak at 3415cm−1 corresponding to OH and NH stretching vibrations of amine groups. The weak band at 2915cm−1 and 2872cm−1 was assigned as the CH stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1653cm−1 and 1570cm−1 belong to NH bending vibrations. The peaks reason at 1143cm−1–1038cm−1 are corresponding to CH bending vibration of the chitosan pyranose ring. These findings are well agreed with the literature of standard chitosan FT-IR measurements [59]. For C-dots, the FT-IR (Fig. 2b) results revealed that and an increase in absorption of amino group at 1653cm−1 and a decrease in adsorption of OH and NH stretching vibrations at 3415cm−1 compared with chitosan. The bands around 1143cm−1–1038cm−1 are associated with the pyranose were completely disappearance [59,60]. These changes may be due to decomposition of the pyranose ring and degradation of the chitosan chain through dehydration. The presence of highly polar groups like amino group improves the hydrophilicity and stability of the C-dots in the aqueous systems. The morphology, size, nature and elemental composition of the prepared C-dots are shown in Fig. 3 . The TEM images of C-dots reveal that the C-dots are homogeneous,and particles dispersed in a spherical shape (Fig. 3(a)). The resulting C-dots diameter ranges from 2 to 8nm with an average size of 4nm (Fig. 3(b)). The XRD pattern measured for the C-dots shows a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ =20° (Fig. 3(c)), which suggest the amorphous nature of C-dots [61]. EDXS identified the elemental composition of C-dots, and the results are presented in Fig. 3(d). These results indicate that the elemental signals of C, N and O are present in C-dots. Additionally, Cu signals were also observed in the EDXS spectra due to the TEM sample holders were made from copper grids.

We further investigated the fluorescent properties of C-dots under various conditions (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. S2(a), the PL intensity of C-dots remains unchanged for 48h at room temperature. It suggests that C-dots show excellent photostability even at long time of incubation. Fig. S2(b) displays the effect of NaCl concentration (0–500mM) on the PL intensity of C-dots. The results indicate that the PL intensity of C-dots remains constant with changing ionic strength. So, the C-dots emission behavior is independent of time and ionic strength of the aqueous medium. This result reveals that the C-dots have a high ability for sensing applications under physiological conditions.

We further investigated the fluorescent properties of C-dots under various conditions (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. S2(a), the PL intensity of C-dots remains unchanged for 48h at room temperature. It suggests that C-dots show excellent photostability even at long time of incubation. Fig. S2(b) displays the effect of NaCl concentration (0–500mM) on the PL intensity of C-dots. The results indicate that the PL intensity of C-dots remains constant with changing ionic strength. So, the C-dots emission behavior is independent of time and ionic strength of the aqueous medium. This result reveals that the C-dots have a high ability for sensing applications under physiological conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH effect on fluorescence sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The pH effect is one of the essential parameters that affects the sensing technique, C-dots PL intensity and quenched PL intensity both are pH dependent. Thus, we employed a series of experiments with different pH values to get appropriate pH, and the results are shown in Fig. 4(a). The PL intensity of C-dots increased when pH was risen from 2 to 4 and a further to some extent its remains the same as from 4 to 6. Afterwards, the pH causes a slight decrease in intensity of C-dots. It shows that the pH range of 4–6 (weakly acidic) was the best range for sensing. The addition of copper ions leads to the quenching of C-dots PL intensity over the full range of pH from 2 to 12. It indicates that the sample pH values highly influence the C-dots quenching efficiency (F 0/F). It could be seen that the optimum (higher PL quenching) pH was 4. Beyond this value, the PL quenching efficiency was relatively lower. The possible causes are described as follows: in highly acidic medium, PL quenching efficiency decreases, and it may be due to the protonation of C-dots surface functional groups such as NH2. These protonated functional groups are arduous to form complexes with the copper ions due to the electrostatic repulsion between the protonated C-dot surface functional groups and the positive charges of copper ions (Cu2+). Similarly, in alkaline solution (pH>7), the copper ions have little influence on C-dots, and thus the PL shows very less quenching. Due to the hydrolysis of copper ions (Cu(OH)2) in alkaline media, it is hard to form a complex with the amine functionalized C-dots. In contrast, copper ions significantly influence quenching efficiencies in acidic medium (pH 4–6). The C-dots quenching efficiencies are more or less to be the same in this range. The optimized pH value is set to 4 for further experiments. At this pH, the C-dots with electron donor groups like, an amino group (nitrogen atoms) donates an electron to copper and makes surface complexation. As well as the availability of alone pair of an electron on the amino group shows its affinity to make a complex with copper ion through electron pair sharing or Lewis acid–base pair.

Next, we investigate the ideal time scale for complexation between copper ions and C-dots. We performed a series of experiments with different time intervals under the above optimized pH. The time-dependent C-dots–Cu2+ complex curve is displayed in Fig. 4(b). These results revealed that 10–15min is required for completion of the reaction between C-dots with the Cu2+ ions.

Sensitivity is one of the most crucial parameter to be examined for the potentiality of the sensing system. For sensitivity study, various concentrations of Cu2+ in the range of 0–5μM (here minimum concentration is 10nM and 0 concentrations is control) were investigated. Fig. 5 shows the steady quenching of C-dots PL intensity at 405nm by increasing the concentration of Cu2+. It indicates that the sensing system is very sensitive to the Cu2+ concentration.

The Stern–Volmer equation explained neither the PL quenching mechanism via neither dynamic nor a static mechanism.
F 0
F
− 1 =
K
sv
C

Here, F 0 and F are the PL intensities of the C-dots at 405nm in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ions, respectively. K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, C is the concentration of the analyte (Cu2+). The Stern–Volmer plot is shown in the inset of Fig. 5. It can be seen that, in a nutshell, all concentrations fit to the linear Stern–Volmer equation indicating that static quenching occur in this current sensing system. It shows a linear quenching with a correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9916 and regression equation y =0.7179x +0.0604. The limit of detection is investigated to be 5nM at the signal to noise ratio of 3. This detection limit is much lower than previous investigations (Table 1 ). These results suggest that the sensing system is highly sensitive to the Cu2+ concentration.

Like sensitivity, to examine the performance of sensing system, another important parameter is selectivity. Fig. 6 shows that the PL quenching changes under similar conditions in the presence and absence of various metal ions like Zn2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Ag2+, Mn2+ and Cd2+. It is observed that the C-dots PL intensity was highly quenched in the presence of Cu2+. On the other hand, no tremendous quenching was observed in the presence of other metal ions [62]. The excellent selectivity was attributed to the Cu2+ has high affinity toward the amine groups on the surface of C-dots. High selectivity and specificity were observed due to the Cu2+ ion has high affinity toward the amine groups on the surface of C-dots than other metal ions.

Recently, it was reported that amine functionalized nanoparticles including C-dots fluorescence quenched by copper ions via inner filter effect and cupric amine complex formation [63,64]. The inner filter effect can be defined as the absorption of the light excitation or/and emission by absorbers might decreases by the emission intensity of fluorophore [63]. Therefore, we also expected inner filter effect and cupric amine complex are possible reasons for fluorescence quenching of C-dots by copper ions.

To confirming this mechanism, we analyzed C-dots solution with and without copper ions by UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. S3, it can be observed that the addition of Cu2+ ions decreases the fluorescence intensity of C-dots,but there is no major effect on the fluorescence wavelength. It may be fluorescence from C-dots can be quenched by copper ions due to complex formation between photo excited C-dots and copper ions [65]. As described above the surface amine functional groups of C-dots have electron donating nitrogen elements (with a lone pair of electrons) that donates electrons to the copper and makes surface complexation through Lewis acid–base pair [66–68]. Here, the lone pair of electrons from amino group's acts as electron donors and copper ions acts as electron acceptors causes to form a cupric amine complex. Beside, this the UV–Visible absorption results indicating that the amine functionalized C-dots have two absorption bands centered at 280nm and followed by 350nm. However, the addition of Cu2+ ions (100μM) into the C-dots solution showed some changes i.e., the absorption band centered at 280nm shifted to 285nm and other broad absorption band observed in the range of NIR region from 540 to 1100nm change in the absorption band centered at 280nm. As discussed above, the synthesized C-dots have abundant amino groups on the surface; thus the changes in the absorption bands due to the cupric amine by the combination of Cu2+ ions with amine groups at the surface of C-dot. Moreover, the absorption bands of C-dots partial overlap with excitation and emission spectra of C-dots. It has been demonstrated that the absorption of the light excitation or/and emission by absorbers might decreases the emission intensity of fluorophore; i.e. inner filter effect. Therefore, the C-dots FL quenching by Cu2+ due to result from the inner filter effect belongs to the specific UV/visible absorption of cupric amine. The TEM results showed that C-dots aggregated in the presence of copper ions it may be due to cupric amine complex formation between C-dots surface amine groups and copper ions (Fig. S4). To further prove that the C-dots FL quenching is related to the formation of cupric amine, control experiments were done using of C-dots without a capping amine, i.e., bare C-dot with hydroxyl-functionalized synthesized from the pyrolysis of citric acid followed by literature [69]. It was found that the FL activities of C-dots without amines were not strongly and specifically quenched by Cu(II) ions (Fig. S5), proving that the formation of cupric amine plays an important role in the selective PL response of the amine-capped C-dots to the Cu2+ ions.

To confirming this mechanism, we analyzed C-dots solution with and without copper ions by UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. S3, it can be observed that the addition of Cu2+ ions decreases the fluorescence intensity of C-dots,but there is no major effect on the fluorescence wavelength. It may be fluorescence from C-dots can be quenched by copper ions due to complex formation between photo excited C-dots and copper ions [65]. As described above the surface amine functional groups of C-dots have electron donating nitrogen elements (with a lone pair of electrons) that donates electrons to the copper and makes surface complexation through Lewis acid–base pair [66–68]. Here, the lone pair of electrons from amino group's acts as electron donors and copper ions acts as electron acceptors causes to form a cupric amine complex. Beside, this the UV–Visible absorption results indicating that the amine functionalized C-dots have two absorption bands centered at 280nm and followed by 350nm. However, the addition of Cu2+ ions (100μM) into the C-dots solution showed some changes i.e., the absorption band centered at 280nm shifted to 285nm and other broad absorption band observed in the range of NIR region from 540 to 1100nm change in the absorption band centered at 280nm. As discussed above, the synthesized C-dots have abundant amino groups on the surface; thus the changes in the absorption bands due to the cupric amine by the combination of Cu2+ ions with amine groups at the surface of C-dot. Moreover, the absorption bands of C-dots partial overlap with excitation and emission spectra of C-dots. It has been demonstrated that the absorption of the light excitation or/and emission by absorbers might decreases the emission intensity of fluorophore; i.e. inner filter effect. Therefore, the C-dots FL quenching by Cu2+ due to result from the inner filter effect belongs to the specific UV/visible absorption of cupric amine. The TEM results showed that C-dots aggregated in the presence of copper ions it may be due to cupric amine complex formation between C-dots surface amine groups and copper ions (Fig. S4). To further prove that the C-dots FL quenching is related to the formation of cupric amine, control experiments were done using of C-dots without a capping amine, i.e., bare C-dot with hydroxyl-functionalized synthesized from the pyrolysis of citric acid followed by literature [69]. It was found that the FL activities of C-dots without amines were not strongly and specifically quenched by Cu(II) ions (Fig. S5), proving that the formation of cupric amine plays an important role in the selective PL response of the amine-capped C-dots to the Cu2+ ions.

In order to sense Cu2+ ions in sea water, first we detect Cu2+ ions in possible sea water co-existence cations such as Na+, K+, Mg2+ and Ca2+, (Fig. S6a) and anions Cl− and SO4 2− (Fig. S6b) at 2μM. These results indicated that there is no major interference from sea water cations and anions. Therefore, feasibility of C-dots to sensing Cu2+ ions are demonstrated from the seawater sample analysis. Different concentrations of Cu2+ in the range of 0–0.8μM were spiked into the seawater samples. The C-dots PL intensity quenching was increased with increasing concentration of copper ions (Fig. 7 ). The inset of Fig. 7 shows the calibration curve for determining Cu2+ in seawater. It was obtained by plotting the values of (F 0/F)−1 versus the concentration of Cu2+. Although the seawater contains many minerals, organics and other components, the developed sensing system still can distinguish the fresh seawater with Cu2+ spiked samples. These results indicate that the sensing system is a powerful and efficient approach to detecting Cu2+ in environmental samples.

In order to sense Cu2+ ions in sea water, first we detect Cu2+ ions in possible sea water co-existence cations such as Na+, K+, Mg2+ and Ca2+, (Fig. S6(a)) and anions Cl− and SO4 2− (Fig. S6(b)) at 2μM. These results indicated that there is no major interference from sea water cations and anions. Therefore, feasibility of C-dots to sensing Cu2+ ions are demonstrated from the seawater sample analysis. Different concentrations of Cu2+ in the range of 0–0.8μM were spiked into the seawater samples. The C-dots PL intensity quenching was increased with increasing concentration of copper ions (Fig. 7). The inset of Fig. 7 shows the calibration curve for determining Cu2+ in seawater. It was obtained by plotting the values of (F 0/F)−1 versus the concentration of Cu2+. Although the seawater contains many minerals, organics and other components, the developed sensing system still can distinguish the fresh seawater with Cu2+ spiked samples. These results indicate that the sensing system is a powerful and efficient approach to detecting Cu2+ in environmental samples.

The prawn shell waste has been successfully used as an efficient strategy for producing fluorescent C-dots, to sensor Cu(II) ions. This method offers many advantages of simplicity, sensitivity, selectivity, economical and it is also a green approach to the environment. Further, C-dots have been used as a novel sensing probe for label-free sensing of Cu2+ ions with the low detection limit of 5nM. The developed sensing system possesses specific binding and high selectivity toward Cu2+ ions and has been successfully used for the analysis of seawater samples. This work also demonstrates the green methodology of fluorescent and biocompatible capability on the nanosize carbon material. The synthesized nano-size carbon materials can be also applied in biomedical applications such as bio-imaging, drug delivery and other clinical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of Taiwan for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.09.065.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Extraction of chitosan from prawn shell wastes and examination of its viable commercial applications

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Gangaraju Gedda received his MS in Analytical chemistry from JNTUH, India in 2010. He is currently a Ph.D student in Doctorial degree program in Marine Biotechnology, NSYSU, under the supervision of Prof. Hui-Fen Wu. He has co-authored over 6 publications in international reputed journals including Green Chemistry, Journal of material chemistry, RSC Advance, Analytical methods and Rapid communication Mass spectrometry. His current research interests including focuses on Synthesis of nanoparticles for various bio-medical, environmental and analytical applications including Drug delivery, Photothermal therapy, and Bio-imaging, Biomolecules and heavy metals detection in environmental samples.

Chun Yi Lee obtained his undergraduate degree in the Department of Chemistry, Fu Jen Catholic University in 2014. Now he is a Master student in the Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-sen University with Professor Hui-en Wu. He is working on nanoparticle synthesis for fluorescence sensing applications.

Yu Chih Lin obtained his undergraduate degree in the Department of Chemistry, National Center University in 2014. Now he is a Master student in the Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-sen University with Professor Hui-en Wu. He is working on nanoparticle synthesis for fluorescence sensing applications.

Professor Hui-Fen Wu currently is a distinguished professor in National Sun Yat-Sen University. She received her Ph.D. degree from the University of Texas at Austin, USA in 1994. Followed by postdoctoral appointments at University of California at Berkeley, she went back to Taiwan as an Associate Professor (1996–2001) and Professor (2001–2006) in the Department of Chemistry, Tamkang University. She joined the Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University in 2006 as a full Professor. Her research work has been focused on Nano-Biomedicine using nanomaterials coupled with Mass Spectrometric techniques, Raman and biosensors. Basically, as an analytical chemist, she has diversified her research interests to the application of nano-based biological mass spectrometry in bacteriology, mycology, biotechnology, oncology and also biosensors in cancer and drug delivery study. In National Sun Yat-Sen University, she is also a co-professor in the Doctoral Degree Program in Marine Biotechnology (2009∼), Center for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (2008∼), Institute of Technology and Medical science (2011∼). She is also a co-professor in the school of Pharmacy, Kaohsiung medical University. She also serves as the editorial board member in more than 10 journals and also serves as an associate editor in Journal of Integrated OMICS. She also served as a director in Taiwan Society of Mass Spectrometry and Taiwan Society of Proteomics. To date, she has more than 190 publications, 6 patents and 11 book chapters.
